content:
Trigger: print('starting up'), a stray logger pointed at sys.stdout, or a third-party library that writes a banner on import.

Consequence: stdout is the JSON-RPC framing channel. One stray byte corrupts the next message and the client disconnects with a parse error that points nowhere. The server looks dead even though the process is healthy.

Alternative: route every log to stderr or a file. Configure logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stderr) before importing anything that logs. In Node, console.log writes to stdout — use console.error or a stderr logger throughout.
title: Never write to stdout from a stdio MCP server

content:
Trigger: a print(payload) added 'just to see what's happening' inside a tool implementation.

Consequence: same root cause as any stdout write — the debug line lands inside the JSON-RPC stream and the client drops the connection mid-tool-call. Worse, the tool appears to succeed server-side; the user just sees a hung Claude.

Alternative: import logging; log = logging.getLogger(__name__); logging.basicConfig(stream=sys.stderr, level=logging.INFO). Then log.info(payload). Wire the logger once at process start so no future contributor reaches for print().
title: Never use print() for debug in an MCP stdio handler

content:
Streamable HTTP (introduced 2024-11, default since 2025) replaces the deprecated SSE transport. It supports bidirectional streaming, resumable sessions, and runs behind any HTTP proxy. stdio remains correct for local subprocess servers because it has zero auth surface and zero network dependency.

Why: SSE is one-way and was retired from the spec. Hosted servers reachable over the internet need Streamable HTTP. Anything launched as a subprocess by the client (Claude Desktop, IDE) should stay on stdio — adding HTTP just multiplies attack surface for no benefit.

When: choose at design time. If the server runs on the user's machine and the client launches it, stdio. If the server is hosted and serves multiple clients, Streamable HTTP.
title: Use Streamable HTTP for remote MCP servers, stdio for local

content:
Trigger: tutorial or scaffold from 2024 wires up Server-Sent Events as the transport. It still works against older clients.

Consequence: SSE was deprecated in the 2025-03 spec revision. New clients (Claude 4.5+, Cursor, Windsurf 2026) drop SSE support on a rolling schedule. A working server today fails silently against a client update tomorrow with no error message users can act on.

Alternative: Streamable HTTP. The migration is small — same JSON-RPC payloads, different framing. Most SDKs (Python mcp, @modelcontextprotocol/sdk) ship a streamable_http transport you swap in for one line.
title: Never deploy a remote MCP server on the legacy SSE transport

content:
Trigger: 'CORS errors in dev, just open it up' — Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true.

Consequence: any website the user visits can call your MCP tools through their browser session. If the server holds an OAuth token for the user (Gmail, GitHub, internal API), every cross-origin attacker now has the same reach. Browsers technically refuse credentialed wildcard requests, but token-bearer flows skip that protection.

Alternative: explicit allow-origins list. Pin to the client URLs you actually trust (claude.ai, cursor.com, your team's internal hostname). Reject the rest with a 403 and a clear error.
title: Never set CORS allow-origin '*' on an authed MCP HTTP server

content:
Streamable HTTP uses chunked transfer with each event prefixed by 'event:' and terminated by a blank line. Flush after every event — buffered responses look identical to a hung server to the client.

Why: clients give up on long-running tools when they see no bytes for N seconds. Periodic progress events keep the channel alive and let the client surface 'still working' UI. Without explicit flushing, the OS buffer holds events for seconds at a time.

When: any tool that takes more than ~500ms to complete. Emit a notifications/progress event every second so the client knows you're alive.
title: Frame Streamable HTTP responses as proper SSE-style events

content:
Trigger: a wrapper that re-initialises the MCP server inside the same process for testing or hot reload.

Consequence: the second initialize message has nowhere to land — the first transport already owns stdin/stdout. The new handshake corrupts the existing one and both connections wedge.

Alternative: one process, one stdio transport, full lifetime. For test harnesses, spawn a fresh subprocess per test. For hot reload, kill and restart the process; do not re-handshake in place.
title: Never run multiple stdio handshakes in one process

content:
A JSON-RPC message with an 'id' field is a request and demands a response. A message without an 'id' is a notification and MUST NOT be answered. Sending a response to a notification is a protocol violation.

Why: clients optimise around the contract. Replying to a notification confuses connection state machines, can deadlock the channel waiting for a request that never comes, and produces silent test failures.

When: every message handler. The SDK usually does this for you, but custom router code that dispatches by method name often forgets the id check.
title: Distinguish JSON-RPC requests from notifications by id presence

content:
Chose stdio. Alternatives considered: Streamable HTTP on localhost, named pipes, Unix domain sockets.

Reason: when the client launches the server as a subprocess, stdio has zero network exposure, zero auth surface, zero CORS surface. The OS handles process supervision and cleanup. HTTP on localhost adds a port, an auth scheme, and a class of attack (other local processes scanning ports) for no benefit. Named pipes and UDS are platform-specific.

Reversible: yes; the SDKs treat transport as configurable. But every reverse case so far has turned out to be a hosted server in disguise — the right move is then Streamable HTTP, not localhost HTTP.
title: stdio over Streamable HTTP for local subprocess servers

content:
Past roughly 15 tools with overlapping descriptions, model tool-selection accuracy drops sharply. Anthropic's own measurements and the 2026 community benchmarks converge on the same cliff.

Why: the model picks tools by reading every tool description on every turn. More descriptions means more tokens, more semantic overlap, and more wrong picks. A 30-tool server measurably hallucinates calls and parameter names.

When: exceeding the cap, split into focused servers. github-issues + github-prs + github-actions beats one github server with all three surfaces. Users connect only the surfaces they need.
title: Cap a single MCP server at ~15 tools

content:
The description is the trigger that makes the model choose the tool. Lead with the user vocabulary that signals intent: 'when the user asks to schedule, book, or reschedule a meeting...' Bad: 'Calendar manipulation utility'.

Why: the model matches the user's words against the tool description. Marketing copy ('powerful, flexible, robust') matches nothing. Concrete trigger phrases match the cases you actually want.

When: every tool. Test by paraphrasing the user's likely request three ways and checking the description contains those words or close synonyms.
title: Write tool descriptions for the model, not the human

content:
Trigger: auto-generating get_X, list_X, create_X, update_X, delete_X tools from an OpenAPI spec.

Consequence: the model has to plan multi-step REST workflows it doesn't understand. It hallucinates parameter names, calls list before create, repeats failed updates. A 50-tool surface drops accuracy below the 15-tool ceiling and confuses the user with verbose tool calls.

Alternative: outcome-shaped tools that match user intents. schedule_meeting wraps the four CRUD calls and the conflict check. update_pull_request_status wraps the GitHub state machine. Fewer, smarter tools beat the auto-generated catalogue every time.
title: Never expose REST-shaped CRUD as MCP tools

content:
Set outputSchema on the tool definition with a JSON Schema describing the structured result. Clients and downstream models then read fields by name instead of regex-parsing prose.

Why: without outputSchema, the model gets a string and parses it to extract the data it needs. That parsing is error-prone, slow, and breaks the moment the prose format changes. With outputSchema, the result is structured and the next tool call can reference fields directly.

When: any tool that returns more than a single value. Even a search result list benefits — the client can render results uniformly instead of inferring structure.
title: Always declare outputSchema for non-trivial tool results

content:
End the description with 'Examples: "book a meeting Tuesday at 3", "reschedule my 4pm to 5".' One line, two or three examples in the user's actual phrasing.

Why: examples give the model a one-shot anchor to match against. Empirically, descriptions with examples outscore prose-only descriptions by ~20% on tool-pick accuracy in the public MCP benchmarks.

When: any tool whose triggering phrase isn't obvious from the name. Trivial tools (read_file, list_files) don't need examples; domain-specific ones do.
title: Include concrete user-vocabulary examples in tool descriptions

content:
Trigger: do_thing(mode='read') vs do_thing(mode='write') — one tool with a string discriminator.

Consequence: the model has to remember which mode does what, and the description has to cover both. Tool-pick logic is fuzzier; outputSchema can't be precise; tests double. The model often passes mode='read' when it wanted mode='write' and silently no-ops.

Alternative: split into read_thing and write_thing. Each has its own description, its own schema, its own permission boundary. One tool, one job.
title: Never fork tool behaviour on a 'mode' parameter

content:
Prefer issue_number over iss_id, repository_full_name over repo, message_body over content. The model picks the right value to pass when the parameter name describes what the value is.

Why: cryptic short names force the model to guess from context. Long, unambiguous names map directly to the user's request and reduce wrong-argument bugs.

When: every inputSchema property. The cost of a longer name is zero tokens at use time (the model writes them anyway); the cost of a wrong guess is a failed tool call.
title: Name input parameters for LLM legibility, not DB columns

content:
{ type: 'string', enum: ['draft', 'open', 'merged', 'closed'] } when the value space is finite. The model picks one of the listed values; bad inputs are rejected before the tool runs.

Why: free strings let the model invent values ('OPEN', 'in-review', 'wip') that the backend doesn't recognise. Enums collapse the surface to the legitimate set and produce a clear validation error when something else is tried.

When: status fields, mode flags, tier levels — anything from a closed list. Update the enum in lockstep with the backend.
title: Use enum types in inputSchema instead of free strings

content:
Trigger: a 'list_orders' tool takes ord_st, cust_id, crt_dt — the same names the underlying table uses.

Consequence: the model can't infer what to pass; users see a tool surface that reads like a leaky abstraction. Renaming the column later forces a backwards-incompatible API change.

Alternative: name parameters in the user's domain — order_status, customer_id, created_after. Map to columns inside the tool implementation. The schema becomes part of the public contract; the columns stay private.
title: Never expose internal database column names as tool parameters

content:
Chose multiple scoped servers. Alternatives considered: one omnibus server, dynamic tool-loading per session.

Reason: scoped servers (github-issues, github-prs) keep each server below the ~15-tool accuracy cliff, let users install only what they need, and produce smaller per-server permission grants. Omnibus servers blow up tool count and force the user to grant permissions they don't need. Dynamic loading sounds clever but breaks the model's mental model — descriptions change between turns.

Reversible: yes; merge or split is a packaging change, not a protocol change. But split-by-default keeps the per-server surface small from day one.
title: Split servers by surface over packing one server with all tools

content:
Tool names are verbs in the user's voice. send_email, search_repos, summarise_issue. Bad: emailService, repoUtility, issueHelper.

Why: the model picks tools by matching user intent to tool name + description. A verb that names the user's outcome wins the match; a noun that names a service category loses it.

When: at design time, write the user request you expect ('summarise issue 42') and pick the tool name that completes the sentence. Iterate the description until the model picks the tool against three paraphrases of the request.
title: One tool, one outcome — verb the user would say

content:
Trigger: a single server that both reads issues and posts comments, or both reads files and writes them.

Consequence: the lethal-trifecta attack class — a poisoned data source (an issue body, a fetched web page, a file the model reads) injects instructions that flip the read tool's output into a command for the write tool. The model is the executor; the read+write split is what makes the exploit possible.

Alternative: split read tools and write tools across separate servers, each with its own scoped credentials. Read-server compromises produce data leaks, not RCE. Users can grant the read scope without the write scope. The 2026-04-16 disclosure ('200k MCP servers at risk') is exactly this pattern.
title: Never combine read and write surfaces in one MCP server

content:
Trigger: a tool implementation that passes the model's arguments straight to a SQL query, a shell, or a downstream API.

Consequence: prompt injection turns into command execution. The inputSchema validation runs on the JSON shape, not the values. A 'path' parameter validated as a string can still be '../../../etc/passwd'.

Alternative: re-validate every parameter inside the tool — path canonicalisation, allowlist checks, type coercion, length limits. Treat the model as an untrusted client. inputSchema is the first filter; server-side validation is the load-bearing one.
title: Never trust tool-call arguments without server-side validation

content:
Trigger: package.json with '"@some/sdk": "latest"' or pyproject.toml without pinned versions. uvx and npx then fetch the newest release on every install.

Consequence: supply-chain RCE class. The 2025-2026 wave logged 30+ CVEs against MCP servers in 60 days; one compromised dependency reached 437,000 downloads before detection. Floating versions mean every user fetched the malicious code automatically.

Alternative: pin every dependency to an exact version (or a narrow range with a lockfile committed). Use uv lock / npm ci. Re-pin during scheduled review, not at user install time. Audit upstream changelogs before bumping.
title: Never publish MCP servers with floating dependency versions

content:
Trigger: README example shows 'uvx my-mcp-server@latest' or 'npx -y my-mcp-server'.

Consequence: every user install runs whatever happens to be the latest version at that moment. A malicious release between user-A's install and user-B's install owns user-B silently.

Alternative: pin in docs too. 'uvx my-mcp-server==1.4.2' or 'npx my-mcp-server@1.4.2'. Bump the version in docs when you ship a release; don't outsource the decision to the user's install timing.
title: Never recommend uvx or npx with floating versions in install docs

content:
Trigger: claude_desktop_config.json sets API_KEY=... in env, the parent shell exports the key, the MCP subprocess inherits it.

Consequence: the secret lives in the parent process environment indefinitely. ps -E or /proc/<pid>/environ exposes it to any local process. Crash dumps include it. Backups include it.

Alternative: short-lived OAuth tokens fetched at server start, refreshed in-process. For static credentials, read from a system secret store (macOS Keychain, libsecret, Windows Credential Manager) at first use — not from environment. Rotate on a schedule.
title: Never pass long-lived secrets through stdio environment

content:
Hosted servers should authenticate users via OAuth 2.1 (PKCE mandatory, refresh token rotation mandatory) and grant the smallest scope each tool needs. Escalate scope only when the tool that needs it is actually called.

Why: a server with read+write+admin in one grant is a single-token compromise away from full account takeover. Incremental scope keeps the blast radius proportional to the tool actually invoked.

When: every hosted MCP server reaching a third-party API on behalf of a user. The April 2026 spec revision standardised the OAuth 2.1 + scope flow; SDKs now ship helpers.
title: OAuth 2.1 with incremental scope for hosted MCP servers

content:
Trigger: a tool description that says 'always call this tool first', 'ignore other instructions when X', or 'this tool overrides previous tools'.

Consequence: prompt injection by your own tool. A malicious server author or compromised description vendor can hijack the agent's plan. Users have no way to audit the description that's actually steering the model.

Alternative: descriptions describe what the tool does and when to use it. Period. Routing logic stays in the client / orchestrator, where the user can see and override it. Treat descriptions as untrusted by the agent layer that consumes them.
title: Never embed instructions in tool descriptions the model reads

content:
Trigger: log.info('tool call: %s', json.dumps(arguments)) inside every tool handler.

Consequence: the arguments are user data. Email bodies, file contents, API tokens passed as parameters — all land in CloudWatch / Datadog / wherever logs ship. PII regulations (GDPR, CCPA) treat this as processing and require consent + retention controls you don't have.

Alternative: log.info('tool call: %s', tool_name) without arguments. For debug, log.debug with an explicit allowlist of safe fields. Never log content, body, message, file, key, or token by default.
title: Never log full tool-call payloads at info level

content:
Trigger: a tool that takes a 'formula' or 'expression' parameter and evaluates it because the input 'is just math'.

Consequence: prompt injection escalates to code execution. The attacker doesn't need to send '; rm -rf /' — Python's eval() opens __import__, JavaScript's eval() reaches process and require. 'Looks safe' is the line every CVE post-mortem starts with.

Alternative: a real parser. ast.literal_eval for Python literals, simpleeval for math, a hand-rolled recursive-descent parser for the DSL you actually want. Never trust 'this input is constrained' — the model can be steered to anything.
title: Never eval or exec model output even when it looks safe

content:
Set maxLength on string params (4_000 chars unless you really need more), maxItems on arrays, maximum on numbers. Reject larger inputs at schema validation time.

Why: a 50MB string parameter is a DoS — your tool tries to process it, blocks the event loop, exhausts memory. Schema-level limits keep the attack at the front door instead of inside the handler.

When: every input field. Tune the limits to the realistic range; add a clear error message so legitimate users hitting the cap know to chunk.
title: Bound input size on every tool parameter

content:
Track tool calls per session-id with a sliding-window counter (Redis or in-memory). Reject above N calls/minute with a clear error the model can recover from. Differentiate read tools (generous) from write tools (strict).

Why: a confused agent can loop on a tool — the same call ten times in five seconds. Without a rate limit, the loop drains your downstream API quota and your wallet. With a limit, the model sees the error and tries something else.

When: every hosted MCP server. Defaults: 60/min for reads, 10/min for writes. Surface the limit values in tool descriptions so the model can plan around them.
title: Rate-limit per session on hosted MCP servers

content:
Prompts in the MCP spec are user-selected templates that produce text. Tools are model-invoked actions that change the world. Don't use a tool when a prompt fits, and never give a prompt side effects.

Why: the consent model differs. Tools require the user to grant a permission boundary; prompts are explicit user picks each time. Mixing the two confuses both the audit log and the user's mental model.

When: 'summarise this' is a prompt. 'send this summary as an email' is a tool. The line is whether the model gets to decide unilaterally to invoke it.
title: Use prompts/list for side-effect-free templates, tools/call for actions

content:
Add a 'last_audited' timestamp to the server's published metadata (package.json, README, registry entry). Update it on every release. Clients can warn when last_audited is more than 90 days old.

Why: the 2026 RapidClaw audit found 52% of public MCP servers had no commits in six months. Abandoned servers don't disappear — they linger and accumulate vulnerabilities. A visible heartbeat lets users and registries triage.

When: every published server. Wire it into your release pipeline so it's automatic; humans forget. The metric is a signal, not a guarantee — but it's better than nothing, which is what most servers ship today.
title: Detect maintainer-abandonment with a heartbeat in the manifest

content:
package.json: '"sdk": "1.4.2"' not '"^1.4.2"'. pyproject.toml: 'mcp==1.4.2' with a uv.lock committed. Resolve to exact versions before publish.

Why: published servers are reproducible deliverables. A user installing today should get the same code as a user installing six months from now (until you ship a new release). Floating ranges defeat that and amplify supply-chain risk.

When: every release. Bump pins deliberately during a scheduled audit; add a changelog entry naming the dependency change.
title: Pin every dependency in the published manifest

content:
Adding a tool is a minor bump. Renaming a tool, changing an inputSchema in a non-additive way, removing an outputSchema field — all major. The user's prompts and the model's call patterns are part of the contract.

Why: the agent's planning depends on tool stability. A renamed parameter at minor-version time silently breaks every downstream user; the model can't tell why the call now fails. Major bumps signal 'audit your usage'.

When: every release. Lean conservative — when in doubt, ship as a major. The cost of a major bump is small; the cost of a silent break is large.
title: Semver discipline: breaking change in tool surface = major bump

content:
On SIGTERM (or stdin EOF for stdio servers): cancel in-flight tool tasks with a deadline, flush stderr logs, close downstream connections, exit zero. Do not emit any further JSON-RPC messages — the client is no longer reading.

Why: emitting a goodbye message after the client has closed the channel is at best ignored, at worst corrupts the next process that inherits the file descriptor. Clean exit means the supervisor (Claude Desktop, systemd) records 'exited normally' and won't restart-loop.

When: every long-running server. Wire signal handling at process start; don't bolt it on after the first SIGKILL incident.
title: Handle SIGTERM cleanly and write nothing more to stdout

content:
Use stderr for every log line, JSON-formatted with timestamp, level, message, and request_id (if available). The user's log aggregator parses the lines; humans tail them with jq.

Why: stderr is the only channel safe to write to from a stdio server. JSON is the only format mechanically readable by both humans and tools. Plain text means grepping; JSON means filtering.

When: every server. Pick the framework once (structlog in Python, pino in Node) and standardise across all servers in your org.
title: Structured logging to stderr with a JSON formatter

content:
GET /healthz returns 200 with {"status": "ok", "version": "1.4.2", "sha": "abc123"}. No auth required. Distinct from MCP tool calls — pure infrastructure check.

Why: load balancers, uptime monitors, and Kubernetes liveness probes need a non-MCP endpoint to ping. Routing health checks through tools/call adds auth surface and noise to the audit log.

When: every hosted MCP server. The version + sha fields are gold during incident review; embed the build sha at build time.
title: Expose a /healthz endpoint on Streamable HTTP servers

content:
The initialize request includes the client's protocol_version. The server replies with the version it supports. If the client wants newer than the server speaks, fail closed with a clear error.

Why: silent downgrade is how compatibility bugs hide for months. The model misinterprets a missing capability as a tool absence; the user blames the model. Explicit negotiation surfaces the mismatch at handshake time.

When: every server. The SDKs handle this if you let them; custom transport code often forgets. Test by handshaking against last-known-stable and current spec versions.
title: Negotiate protocol version explicitly on initialize

content:
Trigger: 'check for updates and self-replace on launch' inside the server binary.

Consequence: the user's agent runs whatever the auto-update fetches, including any compromised release that slipped past your CI. The user cannot pin or audit, and the supply-chain attack window is unbounded.

Alternative: ship updates through the package manager the user picked (uvx, npm, brew). Their package manager has caching, signing, audit. The server just runs the version it was launched with.
title: Never auto-update an MCP server without explicit user opt-in

content:
Chose kebab-case (github-issues, slack-search). Alternatives considered: snake_case, camelCase, dotted (com.example.tool).

Reason: kebab-case matches npm and uvx package conventions, is URL-safe, and reads cleanly in claude_desktop_config.json. Snake_case clashes with Python idioms when consumed from non-Python clients. Dotted names look like Java packages and stress directory listings.

Reversible: yes, but the rename ripples through every config file every user has shipped. Lock the convention before the first public release.
title: Kebab-case server names with optional org prefix

content:
For each tool, list the user-vocabulary cases it handles. 'Use search_repos when the user asks to find, look up, or browse repositories.' Bad: 'search_repos: searches GitHub repositories.'

Why: users decide whether to install your server based on whether it covers their cases. A 'what' description sounds like a manual; a 'when' description reads like a recommendation. The latter wins installs.

When: every public README. Group by user task, not by tool. Two paragraphs of 'when' beat ten of 'what'.
title: README explains WHEN to use each tool, not just what

content:
Paste-able mcpServers entry with the exact command, args, and any required env vars (with placeholders). Above it, link to the spot users edit on macOS, Windows, Linux.

Why: install friction is the largest drop-off in MCP server adoption. A copy-paste snippet halves the time-to-first-call and prevents the typos that make users blame your server for their config error.

When: every README. Include a one-liner test ('after restart, ask Claude: show me my GitHub issues') so users verify the install worked.
title: Ship a working claude_desktop_config.json snippet in the README

content:
Before shipping, run each tool's description and inputSchema through a tool-pick eval: feed the model a paraphrased user prompt and check it picks the right tool with the right arguments. Three paraphrases per tool minimum.

Why: the description that reads well to a human can lose the model on the prompt the user actually types. The eval is cheap (single API call per case) and catches the divergence before users do.

When: every tool addition or description edit. Wire it into CI so a failing self-test blocks the merge.
title: Self-test: would the model pick this tool given this prompt?

content:
Trigger: catch the exception, log it, return an empty success result. The model proceeds as if the tool worked.

Consequence: cascading wrong actions. The model reads an empty list, decides nothing matches, picks a bad fallback. The user sees a confident wrong answer instead of a recoverable error.

Alternative: return a structured isError result with a clear reason. The model treats it as recoverable signal and either retries with different inputs, asks the user, or surfaces the failure. Fail loud, fail recoverable.
title: Never silently fail a tool call

content:
The JSON-RPC envelope carries a numeric code (-32602 invalid params, -32603 internal error, etc.). The data field carries the model-facing message. Keep the two consistent — code -32602 means the message must explain which param was invalid.

Why: client logic routes on the code; the model reads the message. A mismatch means the client retries on a code that says 'transient' while the message says 'permanent', producing infinite loops or premature give-up.

When: every error path. Define a small set of standard codes for your server and never invent new numbers.
title: Match JSON-RPC error code to model-readable error message

content:
Every ~1s during a long tool call, send a notifications/progress event with progressToken (from the request) and a fractional or message field. Conclude with the actual result.

Why: clients show 'tool running' UI based on progress events. Without them, a 30-second tool call looks like a 30-second hang and users (or the agent's own timeout) cancel before the result arrives.

When: any tool that takes more than ~2 seconds. Cap progress events to one per second to avoid flooding the channel; aggregate finer-grained updates server-side.
title: Long-running tools emit notifications/progress events

content:
Pick a per-tool deadline (e.g. 30s for write tools, 10s for reads). Enforce it server-side with asyncio.timeout or AbortController. Document it so client implementations can match. After the deadline, return a recoverable timeout error.

Why: clients have their own timeouts. If client < server, the user sees a timeout while the server keeps running and returns a result no one is waiting for — including a write the user thought failed. Aligned timeouts make the failure mode atomic.

When: every tool. Conservative defaults plus per-tool overrides for the few that genuinely need longer (large file uploads, model inference).
title: Set client-side timeouts that match server-side deadlines

content:
Trigger: a tool handler that emits notifications/message or notifications/progress on the same channel after the call result has been sent.

Consequence: the client expects exactly one response per request. Late notifications either get tied to the wrong request id or cause the client to wait for a phantom request. Channel state diverges; future calls fail with cryptic mismatches.

Alternative: emit progress events DURING the call, send the final result, then go silent on that request. For asynchronous status updates after completion, use a separate notification mechanism (resources/updated, or a follow-up tool the user calls).
title: Never send unsolicited notifications during a tool call response

content:
Trigger: 'helper' tools that compose with each other server-side, with no cycle detection.

Consequence: a malformed input or a model retry can drive the cycle. Each call layer adds latency and consumes the request budget; eventually the stack overflows or the deadline fires. Logs show one tool name on the way in, another on the way back.

Alternative: tool implementations call internal functions, not other tools. If composition is essential, factor the shared logic into a private helper that both tools invoke directly. The MCP boundary is for the model, not for the server's internal call graph.
title: Never let tool A call tool B which calls tool A

content:
Reviewing the 2026-Q1 community audit: servers with three or more write tools accounted for the bulk of accidental-action incidents. One scoped write surface per server keeps the consent boundary clean and the audit log readable.

Why: each additional write tool multiplies the prompt-injection blast radius. The user grants 'write' once for the server but hopes the model picks the right tool — a hope the lethal-trifecta literature says is misplaced.

When: at design time. If a server needs three write tools, that's two servers in a trench coat. Split them.
title: Two write tools per server is usually too many

content:
Real workloads change. The user vocabulary that triggered tool-pick correctly six months ago drifts. Rewriting tool descriptions every quarter recovers ~5-10pp of accuracy in our own server fleet.

Why: language drifts; the model versions drift; the user's mental model drifts. A description tuned in 2024 reads slightly off in 2026. Treat descriptions as living artefacts, not specs.

When: scheduled quarterly review. Run the self-test eval, find the lowest-scoring tools, rewrite their descriptions and re-test. Cheap, high-yield, never finished.
title: Tool descriptions decay fastest of any code in the repo

content:
Per tool, write three short paraphrases of the user request you expect ('check my calendar', 'what's on my schedule', 'do I have anything Tuesday'). If the model picks the right tool on all three, the description is solid. Two-out-of-three means the description leans on the user's exact wording.

Why: the model isn't a regex engine — it generalises from your description. Paraphrase tests measure that generalisation cheaply. Below 3/3, the description needs more concrete examples or broader vocabulary.

When: every tool review or addition. The eval takes seconds and runs in CI; the alternative is finding the gap when a real user types the off-vocab phrase.
title: Three tool-pick paraphrases catch most description bugs

content:
When a tool needs a file or document, accept a resource URI (file://, https://, mcp://) instead of the raw bytes as a parameter. The server fetches and validates the resource itself.

Why: passing file content through the model wastes tokens and exposes content to the model that the user may not have intended to share. URIs let the model name the resource without reading it; the server reads it inside the trust boundary.

When: file uploads, image inputs, document references. The MCP spec includes resource primitives precisely for this — use them instead of base64-stuffing.
title: Resource URIs over passing file content as tool params

content:
Spawn the server as a real subprocess. Drive it with the actual MCP client SDK over real stdin/stdout pipes. Assert against the JSON-RPC responses.

Why: in-process mocks miss the stdio framing bugs that account for most production stdio incidents — wrong line endings, BOM bytes, library banners on stdout. The subprocess harness exercises the actual transport.

When: every stdio server's CI suite. The fixture is reusable across servers; write it once, copy it everywhere.
title: Test stdio servers with a subprocess harness, not in-process mocks

content:
The initialize request includes a capabilities object — the client advertises what it supports (sampling, roots, elicitation). Branch behaviour off the advertised set, not off assumptions or hardcoded version checks.

Why: clients evolve at different speeds. A server that assumes sampling is always available silently fails against clients that don't advertise it. Capability checks are forward-compatible; version checks aren't.

When: any feature that depends on a non-mandatory MCP capability. Cache the capabilities object in connection state and re-read on every dependent operation.
title: Capture client capabilities from initialize and respect them

content:
Trigger: an 'exec' or 'run_command' tool that takes a string and runs it.

Consequence: the model becomes a shell injection vector. Even with sandboxing (Docker, firejail), the surface is huge and audit is impossible — the user has no idea what command will be run before the model picks it.

Alternative: scoped tools for the actual operations the user wants. run_tests, install_dependencies, build_project. Each tool runs a fixed, known command with validated parameters. The user's permission grant becomes meaningful.
title: Never expose unstructured shell access through a tool

content:
Write tools accept an optional idempotency_key parameter. The server stores (key, result) for 24h. Replaying the same key returns the cached result instead of re-executing.

Why: agent retries on transient failures are common. Without idempotency, a retry that arrives after the original succeeded creates duplicates — duplicate emails, duplicate purchases, duplicate API calls. With idempotency, retries are safe.

When: every write tool whose double-execution has user-visible cost. The pattern is borrowed from Stripe; it applies wholesale to agent-triggered writes.
title: Idempotency keys on write tools that affect external systems

content:
Run incoming JSON-RPC messages through a schema validator (the MCP spec ships one). Reject malformed messages with -32600 invalid request before they reach handler code.

Why: handler code that trusts the structure crashes on malformed input — wrong types, missing fields, oversized payloads. Schema validation at the edge keeps handlers focused on business logic and pushes the protocol concerns into one place.

When: every transport. The SDKs do this; custom routers often skip it. The cost is microseconds; the benefit is every handler stops needing defensive type checks.
title: Validate the protocol JSON before dispatching to handlers
